coredata.dc:title: Insight into the relationship of the high photocatalytic performance and double photochromic activity of Z-scheme CsxWO3/AgBr heterostructures under UV–Vis-NIR light utilization
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ning
Gao, Xueyun
Fan, Haikuan
Gao, Yangqin
Ge, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Effective and suitable utilization of solar energy was achieved using a full-spectrum-response photocatalyst AgBr/CsxWO3, applied in the photocatalytic and photochromic field. Benefitting from near-infrared (NIR) absorption ability of CsxWO3, AgBr/CsxWO3 exhibits enhanced visible-near-infrared (Vis-NIR) driven photocatalytic activity. Based on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectra (PL), and the photoelectrochemical analysis, AgBr and CsxWO3 formed a Z-scheme system at the interface to efficiently separate and transfer photo-induced electrons at a fast rate. The quenching experiment and the electron spin resonance (ESR) tests indicated that the photocatalytic reaction is activated by the production of superoxide radicals. Furthermore, the photochromic analysis to different light exhibits the AgBr/CsxWO3 heterostructure is stable under visible light with negligible photodeposition of AgBr, and has excellent double photochromic activity with visible-shielding effect of AgBr to Ag0 and infrared-blocking effect of CsxWO3 under ultraviolet light. This research provides new insights into the manufacture of energy-efficient and environmentally friendly materials that provide excellent performance in both thermal insulation and decontamination.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the AgBr/CsxWO3 composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation process of the AgBr/CsxWO3 nanocomposite.
XRD pattern of AgBr, CsxWO3, AgBr/CsxWO3 nanoparticles.
(a) TEM image of pure CsxWO3, (b) HRTEM image of pure CsxWO3, (c) TEM image of pure AB/CW-70 (d) HRTEM image of AB/CW-70.
XPS spectra of AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70 nanocomposite: (a) Ag 3d, (b) Br 3d, (c) O 1s, and (d) W 4f.

(a). Dependence of photocatalytic activity on the weight of CsxWO3 in the visible light. (b). Relationship between −In(C/C0) and irradiation time of different weight of CsxWO3 in the visible light. (c). Dependence of photocatalytic activity on the weight of CsxWO3 in the infrared (d). Relationship between −In(C/C0) and irradiation time of AB/CW-70 addition amount (e). The absorption spectra of CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70. (f). Cycle experiments of AB/CW-70 for photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation.

Transmittance spectra (350–2600 nm) of (a) CsxWO3, (b) AgBr and (c) AB/CW-70. The transmittances of the whole spectrum and the visible light were respectively measured for 0, 10 min and dark for 1 day. (d) Visible light degradation spectra of untreated and AB/CW-70 powders irradiated with UV light for 12 min; (e) and (f) are the indoor and outdoor infrared thermal imaging schematic diagrams of CsxWO3 (1) AgBr (2) and AB/CW-70 (3) thin film samples.
(a) EIS spectra, (b) Transient photocurrent response curves and (c) PL spectra of AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) the calculated band gap by DRS and (c) Valence band XPS spectra of AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70.
The trapping experiments of the active species for the photocatalytic reaction based on AB/CW-70 photocatalyst.
ESR spectra record of (a) e−, (b) O2-, (c) OH and (d) h+ at ambient temperature with of AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70 photo-catalyst.
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism for photodegradation of RhB based on AB/CW-70 photocatalysts.
The names of all as-prepared samples.

head.ce:title: Insight into the relationship of the high photocatalytic performance and double photochromic activity of Z-scheme CsxWO3/AgBr heterostructures under UV–Vis-NIR light utilization

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
China University of Petroleum Beijing, No. 18 Fuxue RD

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective and suitable utilization of solar energy was achieved using a full-spectrum-response photocatalyst AgBr/CsxWO3, applied in the photocatalytic and photochromic field. Benefitting from near-infrared (NIR) absorption ability of CsxWO3, AgBr/CsxWO3 exhibits enhanced visible-near-infrared (Vis-NIR) driven photocatalytic activity. Based on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectra (PL), and the photoelectrochemical analysis, AgBr and CsxWO3 formed a Z-scheme system at the interface to efficiently separate and transfer photo-induced electrons at a fast rate. The quenching experiment and the electron spin resonance (ESR) tests indicated that the photocatalytic reaction is activated by the production of superoxide radicals. Furthermore, the photochromic analysis to different light exhibits the AgBr/CsxWO3 heterostructure is stable under visible light with negligible photodeposition of AgBr, and has excellent double photochromic activity with visible-shielding effect of AgBr to Ag0 and infrared-blocking effect of CsxWO3 under ultraviolet light. This research provides new insights into the manufacture of energy-efficient and environmentally friendly materials that provide excellent performance in both thermal insulation and decontamination.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, numerous efforts have been made to maximize the use of solar energy, especially Near-infrared (NIR) light to apply in photodegradation [1], water splitting [2,3], CO2 reduction [4,5], and NOx removal [6]. These NIR-driven photocatalysts have only several kinds of nanomaterials, including up-conversion materials/photocatalysts (such as NaYF4: Yb3+, Gd3+, Tm3+@Bi2WO6), phase transition material (such as Cu2(OH)PO4), plasmonic photocatalysts (such as noble metals and non-stoichiometric semiconductors) [7-10]. Among them, tungsten bronze (MxWO3, M likes alkali ions, Bi3+, H+, NH4+), especially Cs-doped tungstate bronze is greatly interesting due to its variation and strong optical absorption ability, such as WO2-NaxWO3 [11], Bi2WO6/BiOI [4], RbxWO3/Ag3VO4 [13], and F-doped NH4(0.33)WO3 [14] in the full-spectrum-responsive photocatalytic application. For CsxWO3, Cs+ can be inserted into the crystal lattice and form the mixed chemical valence of W6+ and W5+, to exhibit strong Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) absorption in NIR range. Meanwhile, CsxWO3 displays a reversible photochromic activity due to the abundant WO6-x units inside CsxWO3 [15]. However, NIR light harvested by CsxWO3 (about 45% of sunlight) can be not effectively utilized due to the low conduction band and low energy transfer efficiency. The previous works have been tried g-C3N4/CsxWO3 [16,17], Nb-doped TiO2/CsxWO3 [18], and CsxWO3/ZnO [19] to realize full-spectrum driven photocatalytic activity by forming a Z-scheme heterojunction. Therefore, selecting a suitable semiconductor is the key to form a heterostructure to the utmost utilization of NIR light in photocatalytic reaction.

Silver halide AgX (X = Cl, Br, I) materials with their extreme photosensitivity have presented extreme photocatalytic potential. Ag/AgX shows a higher photocatalytic degradation under visible light than N-doped TiO2 [20,21]. However, the stability of AgX is a puzzle because AgX, as a photography material, easily absorbs photons to generate electron-holes to photodecompose to Ag0 to do a photochromic reversion [22]. Many studies tried to construct the composite to maintain optical stability successfully. For example, AgBr/WO3, AgBr/TiO2, AgBr/ZnO, and AgBr/g-C3N4 all form a heterojunction with improved photocatalytic performance and stability [23-27]. Due to the matched band structure of AgBr and CsxWO3, these two semiconductors may have some spark effect to enlighten something [23].

Herein, the AgBr/CsxWO3 composites were prepared for the first time by hydrothermal method and in-situ precipitation method. Measurements of their photocatalytic properties show that AgBr/CsxWO3 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic degradation compared with pure AgBr and CsxWO3 under Vis-NIR light irradiation. Meanwhile, the composite combines the photochromic properties of pure AgBr and CsxWO3 to better absorb visible light and near-infrared light after coloration, achieving great Vis-NIR shielding performance. The colored AgBr/CsxWO3 has almost the same photocatalytic activities with the virgin composite. Based on a series analysis of the morphology and photocatalytic mechanism, AgBr/CsxWO3 forms a Z-scheme heterostructure to efficiently separate and transfer photo-induced electrons at a fast rate and becomes an ideal UV-driven photochromic material in full spectrum. This work enlightened how to effectively utilize full-spectrum solar light by combing photocatalytic reaction and photochromic activity.

Tungsten chloride (WCl6, Aladdin, 99.9% A.C.S), Cesium hydroxide monohydrate (HCsO·H2O, Aladdin, 99% A.C.S), acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99.5% A.R.), ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 99.7% A.R.), sodium bromide (NaBr, Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory, 99%), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, Sigma-Aldrich, 99%). All experimental drugs do not need to be purified furtherly. Deionized water is used uniformly in all experiments.

In a typical procedure [29], 0.2976 g of WCl6 was dissolved in 40 mL of absolute ethyl alcohol and then 0.065 g of CsOH·H2O was added into the above solution. After stirring evenly, 10 mL of acetic acid was added and stirred for 1 h to obtain the homogeneous solution. Next, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 190 °C for 20 h. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature, and the precipitate was washed 6 times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C. The obtained samples are blue powder.

The as-prepared CsxWO3 was dispersed to 40 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 1 h. After that, 28.8 mL of AgNO3 (1 mg/mL) aqueous solution was dropwise added to the suspension, and magnetically stirred for 2 h to strengthen the sufficient adsorption of Ag+ ions on the surface of CsxWO3. Finally, 40 mL of NaBr solution (1 mg/mL) was dripped into the reaction system under magnetic stirring, and the mixture was continuously stirred for another 2 h [30]. Different ratios of samples were obtained by changing the amount of CsxWO3 added in the same preparation method, as shown in Table 1 .

The crystal structure of the sample was analyzed by X-ray diffraction with CuK radiation (XRD; Bruker D8 Advance, X-ray diffractometer) at a scanning step of 5°. The morphology of the sample was examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on a PHI 5300 ESCA system with a beam voltage of 3.0 eV and an Ar ion beam energy of 1.0 keV to detect the composition of the final product. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded by a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) of all samples was detected using a Hitachi UV-4100 spectrophotometer, and BaSO4 was used as the reflectance standard. Electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-captured oxidized radicals were obtained on a Bruker-type ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer equipped with a quantum ray Nd: YAG laser as a light source, using UV cut-off filter (nm ≥ 400).

All electrochemical characterizations were performed on a CHI 760e electrochemical workstation using 0.1 M sodium sulfate solution as the electrolyte. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on a CHI 760e electrochemical workstation with an AC amplitude of 10 mV and frequency range between 100 KHz and 0.1 Hz. The measured EIS data were obtained at an applied bias of 0 V vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2 at 25 °C.

For visible light, the photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were investigated by the degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation using the Discover-Multichannel photocatalytic reaction system (PCX50 A Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd.). The 30 mg photocatalyst was sonicated for 10 min to uniformly disperse it in 50 mL of RhB solution (10 mg/L), and then the suspension was placed in the dark and stirred for 30 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that, one 5 W LED lamp (white-light source) was used as the visible light source to irradiate the suspensions under vigorous stirring. The light intensity employed was 80 mW·cm-2. After irradiation, the suspension is collected 4 mL solution every 2 min, the photocatalysts were removed by centrifugation, and the remaining solution was analyzed on the UV–vis spectrometer. For the test of degradation performance under infrared light, a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE 300 UV Xe arc lamp) that filters light using an 800 nm cut-off filter is used as a light source for 180 min, the suspension is collected 4 mL solution every 30 min, and other test conditions are consistent with visible light.

The optical properties were characterized by applying UV–visible spectrometry (HITACHI U-3010) over 350–2600 nm to films made by mixing the nanopowder and resin under the same concentration and then blade coating the composite onto the substrates. The scanning speed was 600 nm min-1. The samples were irradiated by 300 W UV for 0 and 10 min by a xenon lamp. The above operation was also carried out by using a 300 W UV xenon lamp plus a 400 nm filter as a visible light source of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the AgBr/CsxWO3 composites
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , CsxWO3 were prepared under the hydrothermal condition with WCl6 and CsOH·H2O as precursor and acetic acid as shape regulator to change the size of CsxWO3 [18,31]. WCl6 easily hydrolyzes as rich [WO6] and HCl. By adding acetic acid to ethanol, acetic acid has one carboxyl (–COOH) group and is known to form chelates with tungsten species of -O-W-O- linkage in aqueous medium, stabilizing the acidic tungsten solutions and preventing polycondensation and crystallization [32]. AgBr was anchored on CsxWO3 surface by in situ chemical deposition using AgNO3 and NaBr.

XRD was used to investigate the phase and crystallographic structure in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of CsxWO3 can be indexed to pure hexagonal phase of Cs0.32WO3 (JCPDS No. 83–1334) without any other impurity peaks, while the as-prepared AgBr can be indexed to the cubic phase of pure AgBr (JCPDS No. 79–0149). For the AgBr/CsxWO3 composite, the main peaks at 2θ = 23.4°, 27.7°, 33.8°, 36.6°, 49.1° and 56.2° belonged to the (002), (200), (112), (202), (220) and (204) planes of Cs0.32WO3, and the diffraction peaks at 31.0°, 44.4°, 55.1°, 64.6° and 73.2° are corresponding to the (200), (220), (222), (400) and (420) planes of AgBr (cubic phase), respectively, demonstrating the successful formation of the composites. Compared with CsxWO3, no peak shift of AgBr/CsxWO3 is concluded that the deposition process of AgBr did not affect the crystal structure of CsxWO3. Meanwhile, it is obvious that the peak intensity of AgBr is enhanced gradually with increasing the loading ratio of CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70 has the highest intensity of the AgBr peaks. In the coprecipitation, CsxWO3 may provide many active sites to load Ag+ and Br+, thereby achieving CsxWO3 can affect AgBr nucleation and crystallization.

The microstructure and morphology of the obtained samples can be investigated by the TEM images. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the morphology of CsxWO3 is nonuniform, consisting of nanorods (mainly), nanosheets and nanoparticles. In Fig. 3b, the lattice fringe spacing of pure CsxWO3 are 0.39 nm and 0.33 nm, which is consistent with those of (002) plane and (200) plane of CsxWO3, respectively [31,33]. Compared with pure CsxWO3, the surface of CsxWO3 was loaded many irregular nanoparticles in Fig. 3c. In Fig. 3d, these irregular nanoparticles (about 5–10 nm) have the lattice fringe spacing of 0.203 nm and 0.281 nm, belonging to the (220) and (200) plane of AgBr, demonstrating many AgBr particles are anchored in the surface of CsxWO3 [34]. Combining the elemental distribution images in Fig. S1, the result further confirms the successful formation of AgBr/CsxWO3 heterojunctions.

To further analyze the surface and valence states of the samples, XPS has been employed. Fig. S2 demonstrates the composite consists of Ag, Br, Cs, W and O. In Fig. 4 a and 4b, Ag 3d binding energy (BE) peaks of AgBr are located at 367.60 eV and 373.63 eV from Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, while Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 peaks are located at 68.17 eV and 69.19 eV, respectively, which is in good agreement with the reported BE values of AgBr [30]. The O 1 s spectra of pure CsxWO3 (Fig. 4c) can be fitted three peaks: O–H (H2O) at 531.25 eV, oxygen vacancies at 530.46 eV and O-W at 529.87 eV, respectively [16,17]. Fig. 4d shows the W 4f peaks of pure CsxWO3: the higher energy peaks at 35.58 eV from W 4f7/2 and 37.70 eV from W 4f5/2 are attributed to W6+, while the lower energy 34.25 eV from W 4f7/2 and 36.49 eV from W 4f5/2 peaks are originated from reduced W5+ [17]. The rich W5+ and oxygen vacancies both support that CsxWO3 has rich oxygen vacancies. For the AB/CW-70 composite, it can be clearly observed that the peak of Ag 3d and Br 3d all are shifted to the low energy region, while O 1s and W 4f peaks all are shifted to the high energy position. Those peaks’ shift implied the electrons tend to transfer from CsxWO3 to AgBr [35]. The XPS analysis supports the composite can form a heterojunction for the carrier migration.

The photocatalytic performances are evaluated by the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation(λ ≥ 400 nm). In Fig. 5 a, the photocatalytic degradation performance of the AgBr/CsxWO3 composite, especially AB/CW-70 (about 98% degradation within 12 min), is much higher than that of pure AgBr and pure CsxWO3. The photocatalytic performance of the AgBr/CsxWO3 composite sample is AB/CW-70 > AB/CW-80 > AB/CW-40(50) > AB/CW-95 > AB/CW-60 > AgBr > CsxWO3. According to -ln (C/C0) = kt (Fig. 5b), AB/CW-70 possesses the highest rate constant, which is about 5.5 times higher than that of pure AgBr [30].

In addition, the composites demonstrate the NIR-driven photocatalytic degradation under λ > 800 nm illumination. As displayed in Fig. 5c and 5d, AB/CW-70 exhibits good photocatalytic ability after excluding the thermal self-degradation of RhB. For exploring the NIR-driven photocatalytic mechanism, we test the absorption spectra of CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70. In Fig. 5e, CsxWO3 has strong NIR absorption at 900–2000 nm due to the strong LSPR effect caused by rich oxygen vacancies [36]. AB/CW-70 showed a similar NIR absorber with CsxWO3 and an increased visible absorption caused by AgBr. Considering no photocatalytic ability of CsxWO3, the composites can utilize NIR-generated electrons of CsxWO3 to degrade RhB, with the help of AgBr.

The photocatalytic durability of photocatalysts is of vital significance for its practical application. The cycling test was performed by using the same catalyst repeatedly five times under visible light illumination. As shown in Fig. 5f, the catalyst after five cycling tests exhibits a slight decrease and retains 82% of its initial value, indicating the basic stability of AgBr/CsxWO3. As the previous work reported, the decrease may be due to the reduction of AgBr to Ag [24]. However, the Ag 3d XPS spectrum of AgBr/CsxWO3 after photodegradation in 12 min has no Ag peak in Fig. S3. For AgBr/CsxWO3 after photodegradation, the peak of Ag 3d shifts to lower energy than that of AgBr, and to higher energy than that of the virgin AgBr/CsxWO3, which indicates the decrease possibly is due to the electron mobility of the heterojunction.

In order to study the stability mechanism of AgBr/CsxWO3, we explore their photochromic response to different light. Fig. 6 a-c show the transmission spectra of pure CsxWO3, AgBr and AB/CW-70 in a virgin state, colored state after 10 min irradiation and bleaching state after > 1 day, respectively. In Fig. 6a and Fig. S4, the virgin state of CsxWO3 has obvious solar shielding performance with a blue color, due to Cs+ embedded into the interstitial sites of tungsten bronze with a NIR absorption [37]. Under ultraviolet light in Fig. 6a-c and Fig. S4, CsxWO3 colored to deep blue with significantly enhanced NIR absorption ability in NIR region due to H+ into [WO6] structure, AgBr colored to light gray with a visible absorption in the visible region due to the decomposition of AgBr to Ag0 [22], and the transmittance of AB/CW-70 decreases more strongly in Vis-NIR region than those of pure CsxWO3 and AgBr. After one day in the dark, the colored state of AB/CW-70 and CsxWO3 bleach a lot in NIR region, which is a reversible photochromic reaction. However, the special region at 400–600 nm faded slowly, which is ascribed to the photosensitivity of AgBr. The above phenomenon indicates that AB/CW-70 has an obvious double photochromic effect under ultraviolet light. Owing to the decomposition of AgBr under UV light, the colored state of AB/CW-70 formed an Ag/AgBr/CsxWO3 structure. As shown in Fig. 6d, the colored state and the virgin state of AB/CW-70 have similar degradation performances, indicating Ag/AgBr/CsxWO3 also exhibits an excellent photocatalytic activity due to a balance of the destructive heterostructure and the enhanced solar absorption, further supporting the photocatalytic stability.

Meanwhile, under the exposure of visible light, the transmittances of the samples, especially AgBr, are basically unchanged. Meanwhile, under visible irradiation, CsxWO3 also has a little change in transmittance due to its wide bandgap, while the AB/CW-70 transmittance is reduced and the visible region has no change. These indicate that AgBr is relatively stable under visible light, which is consistent with the cycle stability results of previous tests. The analysis represents AgBr/CsxWO3 is suitable to apply in Vis-NIR response photocatalytic performance due to its good stability in Vis-NIR light, and UV-driven photochromic region due to its active response under UV light.

Fig. 6e and 6f are the infrared thermal image of the samples in dark and solar light. Compared with Fig. 6e, the heat and temperature of the films under solar light are increased indicating that AB/CW-70 can absorb much more solar light than that of pure CsxWO3 and AgBr. So the photochromic AB/CW-70 film has a better performance in blocking near-infrared radiation due to the synergy of AgBr and CsxWO3, and can have a greater potential in the field of energy-saving Windows.

In order to investigate the photocatalytic mechanism of AB/CW-70, the photoelectrochemical measurement was tested. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the EIS curves show that AB/CW-70 has a smaller semicircle diameter than pure AgBr and CsxWO3, indicating that AB/CW-70 has a lower charge transfer resistance. The decreasing resistance of AB/CW-70 is attributed to the improved electronic conductivity, implying the heterojunction can realize the effective photogenerated charge transfer and separation [38-41]. The transient photoelectrochemical response measurement (Fig. 7b) displays that AB/CW-70 presents the highest photocurrent response (almost 2.5 and 1.5 folds of AgBr and CsxWO3 at the second cycle, respectively), indicating that AB/CW-70 has the more efficient photogenerated charge generation. That is, AB/CW-70 has the higher separation and transfer efficiency of the photogenerated carriers than AgBr and CsxWO3 [42]. In addition, it should be noted that the pure AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70 samples exhibit a relatively steady photocurrent after five cycles, implying the good photoelectrochemical stability. Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) test was carried out to evaluate the charge separation efficiency. As shown in Fig. 7c, AgBr/CsxWO3 has a lower PL intensity that pure AgBr and CsxWO3, indicating that the introduction of AgBr into CsxWO3 can suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. The above analysis reveals the heterojunction performs an efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers to improve the photocatalytic activity.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared CsxWO3, AgBr and AB/CW-70 photocatalysts are given in Fig. 8 . Pure CsxWO3 has an absorption edge at 400 nm (2.92 eV), which is consistent with the reported bandgap of CsxWO3 [16]. Due to Cs doped, CsxWO3 has an absorption peak in the visible and near-infrared regions, corresponding to the photochromic transmittance of Fig. 6a. AgBr exhibits an absorption edge at 480 nm, suggesting the bandgap is 2.62 eV. Compared with pure AgBr, the absorption of AB/CW-70 significantly increased at the range of 500 nm-800 nm, which is ascribed to the LSPR effect of cesium tungstate [21,29]. Using the Tauc formula, it can be deduced that the band gaps of AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70 complexes (Fig. 8b) are 2.62 eV, 2.92 eV, and 2.89 eV, respectively. Further, the valence-band (VB) edges of AgBr, CsxWO3 and AB/CW-70 were at 1.95 eV, 2.39 eV and 2.21 eV, respectively, according to XPS valence spectra in Fig. 8c. Therefore, the conduction band (CB) of CsxWO3 and AgBr are calculated at −0.53 eV and −0.67 eV, respectively [16].

In order to investigate the main active species in the photocatalytic process, the trapping experiments are conducted by selecting benzoquinone (BQ), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2-EDTA), AgNO3 and isopropanol (IPA) as scavengers of O2 −, h+, e− and OH, respectively [43]. As shown in Fig. 9 , BQ suppressed greatly the photocatalytic degradation efficiency, indicating that O2 − is the dominant active species, while AgNO3 and IPA reduced the photodegradation efficiency of RhB in some degrees, implying e− and OH are the second active species. The introduction of Na2-EDTA is almost useless, which means h+ plays no role in the photocatalytic reaction. The photogenerated h+ always recombines with e− to inhibit the photocatalytic efficiency. The h+ performance exhibits the heterojunction effectively promotes the separation of photogenerated e− and h+, which is consistent with the PL and photoelectrochemical analysis.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) was further investigated to the dominant active species. TEMP is typically used as the radical scavenger of e− and h+. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the signal of TEMPO was discovered in the dark, but no peaks under visible light illumination, showing AB/CW generates a large amount of e− under illumination, thereby consuming a large amount of the capturing agent by converting TEMPO into TEMPOH. For O2- and OH, DMPO is often used as a capture agent. In Fig. 10b and 10c, no characteristic peaks of O2 − and OH were detected in dark, but both of them are strong under visible light irradiation, proving O2 − and OH radicals were produced in the visible photocatalytic process [30]. As can be seen in Fig. 10d, there is a hole signal at g = 2.047 in the complex in the dark, which is the oxygen hole of CsxWO3 [17,44]. Under illumination conditions, the concentration of holes increased, implying the surficial active site increased. Combined with the trapping experiments, it can be concluded that e−, and O2- are the main active species in the photocatalytic process.

The enhanced photocatalytic performance of the composite was mainly ascribed to the interfacial interaction of the heterojunction. On the basis of the above results, the band structure was shown in Fig. 11 a. The conduction band value (ECB) of CsxWO3 and AgBr is more negative than the E0 (O2/O2 −) (−0.33 eV per NHE), indicating that e− can directly reduce the adsorbed O2 molecules into O2 −. The valence band edge potential (EVB) of both CsxWO3 and AgBr is negative than 2.4 eV (OH−/OH) [30], indicating h+ has no effect on the photocatalytic reaction, which is consistent with the trapping analysis. However, OH species play a very small role in the trapping experiment. So it is suitable that OH active species were generated through oxidizing water molecule by O2- (Eqs. (1) and (2)) [45]:
(1) O2 + e− → O2-
(2)
O2- + 2H+ + 2e− → OH + OH−

In the composite, since the VB of AgBr is more positive than that of CsxWO3, the holes transfer to the VB of AgBr theoretically. However, the XPS analysis reveals the surface electron concentration of AgBr increases and that of CsxWO3 decreases, indicating that electrons transferred from CsxWO3 to AgBr [35]. Therefore, the charge transfer mechanism is suitable for the Z-scheme heterojunction. Under visible light, both CsxWO3 and AgBr generated electrons and holes. The photogenerated electrons of AgBr directly reduce O2 to O2-, supported by the trapping experiment and ESR analysis. The recombination of the photogenerated electrons of CsxWO3 and the photogenerated holes of AgBr improves the charge separation rate of AgBr, resulting in the slight decrease of AB/CW-70 in the PL test. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes of CsxWO3 can take part in the oxidation of OH−/OH, which is consistent with the capture experiment.

Meanwhile, we are concerned about the mechanism of NIR-driven degradation in Fig. 11b. The photochromic property and the absorption spectrum reveal CsxWO3 has a strong LSPR effect. Under NIR irradiation, the hot electrons generated by CsxWO3 transferred to the conduction band of AgBr, thereby reacting with O2 to form O2-, achieving the effect of degrading RhB [17]. The performance also reveals the interaction effect of CsxWO3 and AgBr among the Z-scheme heterostructure.

In conclusion, the AgBr/CsxWO3 composite was successfully synthesized by a relatively simple method. The performance of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the visible and near-infrared of the AgBr/CsxWO3 composite was also investigated. The transmittance diagram can well prove that AgBr/CsxWO3 has photochromic performance under ultraviolet light and stable performance without photodepostion of AgBr under visible light. It is shown by DRS that the complex has an LSPR effect and can effectively absorb infrared light, thereby achieving the effect of isolating infrared. Through the band analysis and capture experiments, the photocatalytic mechanism of the AgBr/CsxWO3 complex is a Z-scheme heterostructure, which can effectively improve the utilization of electrons. Due to the synergistic effect of the Z-scheme heterostructure and the LSPR effect, the full spectrum response is achieved, and the performance of photodegradation RhB in full spectrum is achieved. The work indicates AgBr/CsxWO3 is suitable to be the UV-driven photochromic and Vis-NIR driven photocatalytic material, to utilize solar light in a new insight.

Ning Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xueyun Gao: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Haikuan Fan: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Yangqin Gao: Investigation, Visualization. Lei Ge: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No. 2462017YJRC048, 2462018BJC005), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51802351).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
